does: This paper investigates the latent representations of the Multitrack Music Transformer and develops inference-time activation steering methods to control discrete musical attributes without retraining. The authors use Difference-in-Means methodology to isolate latent directions for Pitch and Duration within the model's residual stream, then validate the Linear Representation Hypothesis by demonstrating high correlation between steering magnitude and attribute shift. To handle interference when controlling multiple attributes simultaneously, they introduce a Dual Steering framework that applies Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalization to geometrically decouple conflicting feature directions. Experiments show this orthogonalized approach reduces conceptual interference and signal degradation compared to naive vector addition, enabling independent modulation of multiple attributes in generated symbolic music.
connects: This work builds on recent mechanistic interpretability research that reveals how neural networks encode semantic information in their latent representations, extending approaches like activation patching and steering from language models to the music domain. It connects to prior work on disentangled representations in generative models and responds to the broader challenge of achieving fine-grained control in transformer-based sequence generation. The Linear Representation Hypothesis that the paper validates draws from foundational studies on the geometry of neural representations, while the orthogonalization strategy for multi-attribute control relates to work addressing feature entanglement in high-dimensional latent spaces. The focus on symbolic music generation with Transformers builds directly on advances in the Multitrack Music Transformer architecture and extends interpretability methods previously developed for language and vision domains.
enables: This work opens pathways for interpretable and controllable symbolic music generation without expensive retraining cycles, enabling musicians and researchers to iteratively explore attribute variations at inference time. The mechanistic understanding of how music transformers encode specific attributes could inform design of better architectures with inherently more disentangled representations. The Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization framework generalizes beyond music, potentially offering a principled approach for multi-attribute steering in other sequence generation tasks like text or protein design. Future work could extend the method to additional musical attributes beyond pitch and duration—such as dynamics, tempo, or timbre—and investigate whether the discovered steering directions transfer across different model architectures or training regimes, potentially revealing universal geometric principles of how transformers represent structured symbolic information.
